content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 207 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Executive Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660     322-5660        322-5601        322-6441        322-6444


March 14, 1979


Mr. Robert R. Stein
The Stein Company
6460 Havenside Drive
Sacramento, California 95831

Dear Mr. Stein:

The Commission staff has considered the questions posed in your
letter dated February 12, 1979. Your questions pertained to
Government Code Section 86205(i) which states that no lobbyist
shall "accept or agree to accept any payment in any way contingent
upon the defeat, inaction or outcome of any proposed legislative
or administrative action."

In response to your first question, there is nothing in the Act
which prevents a person from functioning as both a real estate
broker and a registered lobbyist.

However, there may be instances where the prohibition of
Section 86205(i) may prevent a person from serving both as
broker and lobbyist with regard to a particular transaction,
because of the hypothetical nature of the questions you have
posed, we cannot provide definitive answers.

You should note that the terms legislative and administrative
action do not include all actions of every state agency.
Government Code Section 82011 defines legislative action to mean
"the drafting, introduction, consideration, modification,
enactment or defeat of any bill, resolution, amendment, report,
nomination or other matter by the legislature or by either house
or any committee, subcommittee, joint or select committee thereof,
or by a member or employee of the legislature acting in his
official capacity." "Legislative action" also means the action of
the Governor in signing or vetoing any bill."

Mr. Robert R. Stein                    -2-                    March 14, 1979


Government Code Section 82092 defines "administrative action" to
mean "the proposal, drafting, development, consideration,
amendment, enactment or defeat by any state agency of any rule,
regulation or other action in any rate-making proceeding or any
quasi-legislative proceeding, which shall include any proceeding
governed by Chapter 4.5 of Division 3 of Title 2 of the Government
Code (beginning with Section 11371)."

By regulation the Commission has determined that the following
proceedings are not "administrative actions":

1. A proceeding to determine the rights or duties of a
   person under existing laws, regulations or policies.

2. A proceeding involving the issuance, amendment or revocation
   of a permit, license or other entitlement for use.

3. A proceeding to enforce compliance with existing law or to
   impose sanctions for violations of existing law.

4. A proceeding at which an action is taken involving the
   purchase or sale of property, goods or services by such agency.

5. A proceeding at which an action is taken which is ministerial
   in nature.

6. A proceeding at which an action is taken awarding a grant
   or contract.

7. A proceeding involving the issuance of a legal opinion.
   (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18202)

Subsection (4), cited above, excludes from the definition of administrative
action "a proceeding at which an action is taken involving
the purchase of property, goods or services by such agency."
Therefore, the prohibit of Section 86205(c) would not apply to
a situation where the activity of a broker was limited to negotiating
a contract of sale or purchase with the state on behalf of a client.

Please contact me if I can be of further assistance.

Sincerely,

[Signature]

J. RICHARD EICHMAN
Special Compliance Representative

THE STEIN COMPANY
REAL ESTATE DEVELOPMENT • LEASING
COMMERCIAL INVESTMENTS • CONSULTING

SIXTY-FOUR EIGHTY-HAVENSIDE DRIVE • SACRAMENTO CALIFORNIA 95831 • AREA CODE 916

February 12, 1979

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Attention: Mr. Richard Eichman

As you suggested when we talked on the phone,

MR. EICHMAN,

concerning my question last Thursday, I am putting it in writing
so that the Commission staff may analyze it and hopefully give me
an answer by letter.

My question relates to a part of the Government Code, Section
86205 (f). I'm raising the question because of the June 1976
Instruction Manual for Lobbyists which states under VIII. Lobby-
ist Prohibitions "(f) accept or agree to accept any payment in
any way contingent upon the defeat, enactment or outcome of any
proposed legislative or administrative action."

I have been a licensed real estate broker in California since approximately 1963. During the 1977-78 session of the legislature
I was a registered Lobbyist. The registration was terminated
November 15, 1978.

My questions are: Section 86205 (f) of the Government Code notwithstanding:

1. May a licensed real estate broker register and function
as both a real estate broker and as a registered lobbyist?

2. Since most real estate commissions are earned on a
'contingent' basis, can a broker registered as a lobbyist accept an assignment involving real estate purchase
or sale which requires defeat or enactment of legislation or outcome of any proposed legislative or administrative action?

Fair Political Practices Commission
February 12, 1979
Page 2.


3. If yes to No. 2, are there any limitations or special
conditions which govern?

The questions above are made upon the assumption that the broker-
lobbyist would comply with all other provisions of the law appli-
cable to lobbyists and brokers.

To present arguments in support of an affirmative answer to No. 2,
let me suggest a couple of hypothetical situations which could
very well occur.

Situation A - A local community wants a State Park. The property
owner seeks the assistance of a lobbyist to facilitate the acqui-
sition. The lobbyist is also a real estate broker who has worked
out the disposition of the purchase proceeds with the property
owner who has agreed to invest them in the development of a com-
mercial-industrial project. A contract between the broker-lobbyist
and the property owner provides for payment of a commission or fee
for handling the acquisition by the State, the development, issuing and subsequent sale of the commercial-industrial complex. All
reporting requirements of the lobbyist required under State law
are complied with.

Situation B - The State has already indicated acquisition of a
property owned by a private party. A broker-lobbyist is engaged
by the property owner to represent him and expedite the transac-
tion with the State. The broker enters into a contract in which
the fee is contingent upon the State's acquisition of the owner's
property. The broker presents the owner another property during
the negotiations with the State and suggests he purchase it with
the proceeds from the State's purchase of the first property. The
owner does so and the broker is paid a contingent fee by the owner
of the second property, having fully complied with the disclosure
provisions of the real estate law and the reporting requirements
of the FPPC.

One can think up literally hundreds of variations on the theme
of a broker-lobbyist representing one or more owners, attempting
to influence legislation or administrative rules or the interpre-
tation of same to the advantage of his clients. If real estate
is involved, a real estate broker's license is most likely going
to be required by State law. Most always fees paid to brokers
are on a 'contingent' basis, yet legislative advocacy may well also
be involved to consummate the transactions. Should the property
owners view the transactions as ones in which they are not will-
ing to invest prepaid fees, yet the broker-lobbyist is willing to
finance the effort with his own time and money on a 'contingent'

Fair Political Practices Commission
February 12, 1979
Page 3.


fee basis it appears from reading your manual and the real estate
laws there there may be some conflict between the two.

I wish to pursue the development of a service to clients as a
broker-lobbyist to take advantage of my years of experience in
both fields, but I do not want to be in violation of either the
word or the intent of the law.

I would plead that if a broker is properly licensed and properly
registered as a lobbyist, the public interest will not be ad-
versely impacted if thru open advocacy the legislature or an ad-
ministrative agency is convinced that it is in the public interest
for the State to acquire a particular piece of real estate whether
the fee prepaid or contingent.

I would appreciate learning of your interpretation.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
ROBERT R. STEIN

RRS/js

sections.question: 1. May a licensed real estate broker register and function as both a real estate broker and as a registered lobbyist? 2. Since most real estate commissions are earned on a 'contingent' basis, can a broker registered as a lobbyist accept an assignment involving real estate purchase or sale which requires defeat or enactment of legislation or outcome of any proposed legislative or administrative action? 3. If yes to No. 2, are there any limitations or special conditions which govern?
sections.conclusion: In response to your first question, there is nothing in the Act which prevents a person from functioning as both a real estate broker and a registered lobbyist. However, there may be instances where the prohibition of Section 86205(f) may prevent a person from serving both as broker and lobbyist with regard to a particular transaction. Because of the hypothetical nature of the questions you have posed, we cannot provide definitive answers. The prohibition of Section 86205(f) would not apply to a situation where the activity of a broker was limited to negotiating a contract of sale or purchase with the state on behalf of a client.
sections.question_synthetic: Can a person function as both a licensed real estate broker and a registered lobbyist, and specifically, can such a person accept contingent fee compensation on real estate transactions that involve lobbying for legislative or administrative action?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that a person may function as both a broker and lobbyist, but Section 86205(f) may prohibit contingent fee arrangements in certain transactions. The prohibition does not apply when broker activity is limited to negotiating property purchase or sale contracts with state agencies, as such proceedings are excluded from the definition of administrative action.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both incoming request (dated February 12, 1979) and FPPC response (dated March 14, 1979). FPPC response is the primary document. Incoming request provides extensive hypothetical scenarios and context. FPPC provides limited definitive guidance due to hypothetical nature of questions but identifies specific regulatory exclusions from administrative action definition that would permit broker activity.

QUESTION: 1. May a licensed real estate broker register and function as both a real estate broker and as a registered lobbyist? 2. Since most real estate commissions are earned on a 'contingent' basis, can a broker registered as a lobbyist accept an assignment involving real estate purchase or sale which requires defeat or enactment of legislation or outcome of any proposed legislative or administrative action? 3. If yes to No. 2, are there any limitations or special conditions which govern?

CONCLUSION: In response to your first question, there is nothing in the Act which prevents a person from functioning as both a real estate broker and a registered lobbyist. However, there may be instances where the prohibition of Section 86205(f) may prevent a person from serving both as broker and lobbyist with regard to a particular transaction. Because of the hypothetical nature of the questions you have posed, we cannot provide definitive answers. The prohibition of Section 86205(f) would not apply to a situation where the activity of a broker was limited to negotiating a contract of sale or purchase with the state on behalf of a client.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to Robert Stein's inquiries about whether a person can simultaneously serve as a real estate broker and registered lobbyist while receiving contingent fees on transactions involving legislative or administrative action. The FPPC clarified that dual roles are permissible, but contingent compensation may be prohibited depending on the nature of the transaction, with an exception for state property purchase or sale transactions.
